title: One place instead of three
checkLabels.t3: The heading is still dark red
checkLabels.t5: Not a single style attribute left
checkLabels.t7: The block is in the head
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of moving something. Bring the code back.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. The three style attributes are still there.
hint2.untouched: First remove the three style attributes. The page will go black — and that's where the work starts.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: <style> is closed with </style>. Like every other tag.
hint3.not-closed: <style> p { color: teal; } — the </style> is missing here. The browser keeps reading and turns the whole page into stylesheet.
hint4.not-closed: Check that the style block has both an opening and a closing tag.
why.no-h1: There is no heading. You deleted the <h1> instead of moving its colour.
hint2.no-h1: You're moving where the colour is written. You're not removing elements.
why.no-p: There is not a single paragraph left. You deleted them.
hint2.no-p: The two lines of text have to stay. Only their colour moves elsewhere.
why.h1-lost-color: The heading isn't dark red any more. You removed the style attribute, but your new rule doesn't reach it.
hint2.h1-lost-color: A rule starts with a name. That name says which elements to touch. If it only says p, the heading isn't one of them.
hint3.h1-lost-color: h2 { color: navy; } colours every h2 on the page. Only those. An h3 knows nothing about it.
hint4.h1-lost-color: You need two rules — one for h1, one for p. Or one that covers both.
why.p-lost-color: A paragraph is left without colour. The check looks at both, not the first one.
hint2.p-lost-color: The p rule isn't about one paragraph. It's about all of them.
hint3.p-lost-color: If you singled out the first paragraph somehow, undo it. The whole point here is that one rule catches all of them at once.
hint4.p-lost-color: One rule for p. Nothing more. Both paragraphs will colour themselves.
why.still-inline: At least one style attribute is still on a tag. The target is zero — the colour should live in one place.
hint2.still-inline: Search the code for the word style followed by an equals sign. There should be none.
hint3.still-inline:
You may have removed only one

. There were three attributes — the heading and both paragraphs.
hint4.still-inline: Delete style="color: darkred" from all three tags. The rest of them stays as it is.
why.no-style-tag: There is no style block. You removed the attributes but never wrote the rule — that's why the page is black.
hint2.no-style-tag: Rules live in a <style> tag. It doesn't show on the page — it says how everything else should look.
hint3.no-style-tag: <style> h2 { color: navy; } </style> — the tag is the wrapper, the rule sits inside it.
hint4.no-style-tag: Add a <style> inside <head> and write the rules in it.
why.style-in-body: The style block is outside <head>. It works — the browser is forgiving and will run it anywhere. But that isn't where it belongs.
hint2.style-in-body: <head> is information ABOUT the page. <body> is what people see. Styles aren't seen.
hint3.style-in-body: Put the styles at the end of the page and the browser paints everything black first, then repaints it. On a slow connection people see the flash.
hint4.style-in-body: Move the whole <style> block between <head> and </head>.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. You're moving the colour, not deleting text.
hint2.lost-p: Both sentences under the heading should be there.
blocks.0.text: Last lesson you wrote the same thing three times. Three tags, three times color: darkred.
blocks.1.text: Now the cafe owner says she wants green. Count how many places you'd edit. Three — on a page with three elements. A real site has two hundred. And tomorrow she'll change her mind again.
blocks.4.text: p is a selector — it says which elements. Inside the curly braces is the same thing you wrote in the tag: property, colon, value. A semicolon at the end.
blocks.5.text: The difference is that this rule catches ALL paragraphs at once. One, ten, two hundred — it doesn't matter. Change the colour here and it changes everywhere.
blocks.7.text: <head> is information about the page. <body> is what people see. Styles aren't content — they're instructions on how content should look. That's why they belong in the head.
blocks.8.text: Put them in the body and it will work. The browser will run them. But someone on a slow connection will see the page black first, then repainted — because the styles arrived after the text.
blocks.9.text: Remove the three style attributes. Do the same job with one rule in <head> — the heading and both paragraphs stay dark red.
blocks.10.text: The style attribute isn't forbidden. It's just almost never the right choice. There's one more place the colour can live — a whole separate file. We look at that in two lessons.
